content:
type: SURVEY
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
note: Adding with_counts param to groupby filter: sync_do_groupby() and async do_groupby() functions, plus _GroupTuple class and filters dict entry.

fields.note: Adding with_counts param to groupby filter: sync_do_groupby() and async do_groupby() functions, plus _GroupTuple class and filters dict entry.

content:
type: DRAFT files: src/jinja2/filters.py region: sync_do_groupby() and do_groupby() functions code: @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can also use the ``case_sensitive`` parameter to control whether string comparisons should be case-sensitive when grouping. By default, strings are compared case-insensitively. Set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group. ``groupby`` will then yield ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ] if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out note: Merged construct combining case_sensitive and with_counts params. Please compare and confirm/adjust.

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja

  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}

type: DRAFT files: src/jinja2/filters.py region: sync_do_groupby() and do_groupby() functions (full merged version) code: @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
By default, strings are compared case-insensitively. Set ``case_sensitive`` to ``True`` to compare case-sensitively. Set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group. ``groupby`` will then yield ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) values = await auto_to_list(value) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): group_values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_values[0]), group_values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out note: Combined your case_sensitive approach (expr for grouper display, sort_expr for case-insensitive sort/group) with my with_counts addition. Docstring covers both params. Please confirm this matches or propose changes.

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
By default, strings are compared case-insensitively. Set ``case_sensitive`` to ``True`` to compare case-sensitively. Set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group. ``groupby`` will then yield ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) values = await auto_to_list(value) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): group_values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_values[0]), group_values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out
fields.note: Combined your case_sensitive approach (expr for grouper display, sort_expr for case-insensitive sort/group) with my with_counts addition. Docstring covers both params. Please confirm this matches or propose changes.

type: AGREE files: src/jinja2/filters.py region: sync_do_groupby() and do_groupby() functions code: @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
By default, grouping is case-insensitive, so ``"CA"`` and ``"ca"`` end up in the same group (using whichever casing appeared first). Pass ``case_sensitive=True`` to treat different casing as distinct groups. Set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group. ``groupby`` will then yield ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=sort_expr), sort_expr): values = await auto_to_list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out note: Confirmed, adopting your exact text verbatim for byte-identical merge.

    • {% endfor %}
By default, strings are compared case-insensitively. Set ``case_sensitive`` to ``True`` to compare case-sensitively. Set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group. ``groupby`` will then yield ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) values = await auto_to_list(value) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): group_values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_values[0]), group_values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out

type: AGREE files: src/jinja2/filters.py region: sync_do_groupby() and do_groupby() functions (FINAL - locked) code: @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
By default, strings are compared case-insensitively. Set ``case_sensitive`` to ``True`` to compare case-sensitively. Set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group. ``groupby`` will then yield ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) values = await auto_to_list(value) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): group_values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_values[0]), group_values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out note: Confirmed byte-identical to your last message. This is the final locked text. Proceeding to implement verbatim now.

The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}

    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja

  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
By default, strings are compared case-insensitively. Set ``case_sensitive`` to ``True`` to compare case-sensitively. Set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group. ``groupby`` will then yield ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja

Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) sort_expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, ) values = await auto_to_list(value) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=sort_expr), sort_expr): group_values = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_values[0]), group_values)) if with_counts: return [(grouper, items, len(items)) for grouper, items in out] return out
